critic_raw:
This ensemble of XGBoost on structured data plus logistic regression on clinical notes has several critical methodological flaws.

1. TF-IDF ON DISCHARGE NOTES IS A WEAK PROXY FOR CLINICAL NOTE INFORMATION AND BIASES THE ENSEMBLE. The clinical note component uses TF-IDF with 500 features over lowercased, de-identified text. TF-IDF on bag-of-words loses all syntactic structure, negation ('no fever' and 'fever' become similar), and clinical context. More critically, discharge notes are written after the clinical decision to discharge has been made -- they summarize the admission and can contain implicit risk signals ('patient discharged against medical advice,' 'social support limited') that a reader would associate with readmission risk. Whether these signals are legitimate predictors (available at discharge) or reflect the admitting team's implicit readmission risk assessment (which would make the feature partially tautological) is not established.

2. ENSEMBLE WEIGHTS ARE TUNED ON THE VALIDATION SET, CREATING A HYPERPARAMETER THAT ABSORBS VALIDATION VARIANCE. The ensemble combines Model A and Model B via a weighted average where weights are tuned on the validation set. With only two components, the ensemble weight is a single scalar -- but it is specifically selected to maximize AUROC on the validation set. In a 20% validation set of ~10,000 records (2,000 records), optimization of this scalar will capture validation-specific noise. The final reported test AUROC cannot be cleanly interpreted because the ensemble weight was tuned to the validation set performance, not to a held-out set.

3. THE BASELINE DOES NOT ISOLATE THE CLINICAL NOTES CONTRIBUTION FROM THE ENSEMBLE METHODOLOGY. The baseline is logistic regression on structured data only. The treatment is XGBoost on structured data plus logistic regression on notes, combined via weighted average. If the ensemble wins, there are two candidate explanations: (a) clinical notes add predictive information and (b) XGBoost outperforms the logistic regression baseline on structured data alone. The design does not include a standalone XGBoost baseline, making it impossible to decompose the improvement.

4. TEMPORAL STRATIFICATION BY READMISSION CLASS WITHIN TEMPORAL WINDOWS DISTORTS PREVALENCE-BASED METRICS. Within each temporal segment, the design stratifies by readmission class to preserve label distribution. This is contradictory to the stated motivation for temporal splitting: if readmission rates genuinely evolve over time (e.g., due to discharge practice changes), forcing identical class distributions across splits removes this temporal drift from evaluation. AUROC is unaffected by class prevalence, but any calibration-sensitive metric or operating point analysis will be distorted.

5. CLINICAL NOTES PREPROCESSING CHAIN IS UNDERSPECIFIED FOR REPRODUCIBILITY AND LEAKAGE CHECKING. The design specifies lowercasing, de-identification, tokenization, and TF-IDF vectorization, all fit on training data. But de-identification of PHI patterns (names, dates, locations) involves rule-based or model-based substitution -- if dates are de-identified by replacement with relative time offsets computed from the discharge date, the de-identification process itself may embed temporal distance information that is predictive of readmission. This is a form of label-adjacent feature engineering that is not acknowledged.
all_issues_raised: TF-IDF on discharge notes loses negation and context, and note content may partially encode implicit clinician readmission risk assessment
Ensemble weight tuned on validation set absorbs validation-specific noise, invalidating clean test set interpretation
Baseline does not include standalone XGBoost, preventing decomposition of note contribution vs. model class advantage
Within-temporal-window readmission stratification removes temporal prevalence drift from evaluation, distorting calibration analysis
De-identification process may embed date-relative temporal signals that are predictive of readmission
Ensemble weights tuned on same validation set used for component model selection; double-dip creates compounded selection bias
TF-IDF limited to 500 features truncates vocabulary systematically; rare clinical terms may be excluded in ways that affect specific patient subgroups
Baseline uses structured features only; ensemble gain cannot be attributed to combination effect vs. XGBoost nonlinearity vs. note information
AUROC as primary metric treats all operating points equally; improvement at clinically irrelevant thresholds may drive the headline number
Subgroup fairness analysis lacks multiplicity correction; false positives from multiple comparisons not controlled
Validation set used for three sequential optimization steps (XGBoost tuning, LR tuning, ensemble weight tuning) increasing overfitting risk
TF-IDF with 500 features captures high-frequency medical terminology already present in structured features, not the social and functional content that makes notes useful
Baseline comparison does not isolate whether improvement comes from XGBoost vs. logistic regression or from clinical notes — XGBoost alone would likely beat the baseline
Planned vs. unplanned readmission classification is unreliable in EHR coding, contaminating the positive class
AUROC does not validate the resource-constrained intervention use case where precision at operational thresholds matters
